meta.title: Colour development guides
meta.note: These guides focus on the moving parts behind colour: host glaze, body, thickness, cooling, and chemistry. They are meant to help plan tests, not promise exact fired colour.

families.name: Clear host selection
families.goal: Use transparent bases intentionally instead of treating every clear as interchangeable.
bestHosts: G2926B for stable general cone-6 hosting
G1214M for sensitive warm colours and chrome-tin routes
G1216M or G3838A for tight porcelain fit
G3879 for maximum low-fire clarity
colourants: stains and underglaze layers usually show more honestly under a clear than inside a reactive base
tiny host differences matter most in warm colours and near-white tints
bodyEffects: white stoneware and porcelain preserve colour purity better than warm buff or red bodies
red earthenware always contributes warmth under a clear, even under a very neutral host
thicknessEffects: one extra coat can be the difference between clean colour and clouded decoration
clarity problems at low fire are often thickness problems before they are chemistry problems
coolingEffects: stable clears usually change less with cooling than reactive families
slow cooling can still soften transparency or encourage a slight milkiness in some matte-adjacent hosts
commonMistakes: adding opacifier to a clear host and then wondering why the colour looks dead
testing a warm stain in the wrong clear and blaming the stain number
trying to solve a host problem by adding more colourant
stepSequence: pick the clear based on fit first and colour role second
fire the clear alone on the target body
put the same colour system under two or three host clears before committing

families.name: Celadon green
families.goal: Build a light iron transparent that reads green instead of muddy amber.
bestHosts: clean transparent hosts with little or no opacifier
Ravenscrag celadon branches or stable cone-6 clears on very light bodies
colourants: low iron loads usually create the tint rather than a saturated iron effect
iron must stay low enough that the host remains transparent
bodyEffects: porcelain and very white stoneware show the clearest celadon family
warmer stoneware shifts the same recipe toward olive or amber
thicknessEffects: thin sections often look almost clear while pools go green
too much thickness pushes the family toward olive, amber, or cloudy green
coolingEffects: gentle cooling can deepen the green slightly in some hosts
the body effect is usually stronger than the cooling effect
atmosphereEffects: oxidation celadons at cone 6 read cleaner and lighter
reduction high-fire celadons can read deeper and more traditional
commonMistakes: using a dark or speckled body and expecting a pure celadon
adding rutile or opacifier and then wondering why the result is no longer celadon
raising iron to fix weak colour instead of improving the host and thickness
stepSequence: start with a clean transparent host
test low iron increments on a white body
promote the lowest iron amount that still gives a visible green pool

families.name: Iron amber and honey
families.goal: Use iron as a warm transparent colourant instead of pushing straight into temmoku or rust.
bestHosts: Alberta Slip and similar warm natural bases
clear or semi-transparent bases with enough melt to stay glassy
colourants: small iron additions warm a transparent host into amber or honey
natural iron-bearing slip bases can reach amber with little or no extra colourant
bodyEffects: white bodies show a clearer honey read
iron-bearing bodies push the family darker and browner
thicknessEffects: thin edges can stay tea-clear while pooled areas go amber
heavy thickness quickly pushes the family toward brown
coolingEffects: slow cooling often deepens warmth and variation
faster cooling can keep the amber cleaner and more glassy
commonMistakes: overloading iron when the real goal was transparency plus warmth
choosing a host that is too matte to let the colour glow
ignoring the body contribution in natural-base systems
stepSequence: start from the warm clear or natural base first
test thickness on a vertical tile
stop at the first amber zone before the glaze goes brown and saturated

families.name: Cobalt blue
families.goal: Control blue saturation before cobalt flattens the whole glaze.
bestHosts: stable transparent gloss for clean blue
reactive hosts when the goal is breaking, mottling, or depth rather than flat blue
colourants: small cobalt additions show immediately
cobalt pairs well with rutile, iron, or tin when a more complex blue is wanted
bodyEffects: white bodies keep cobalt brighter and cleaner
warm bodies mute or green the blue slightly
thicknessEffects: thin cobalt can read elegant and transparent
heavy cobalt often reads flat, heavy, or overly dark before it reads more beautiful
coolingEffects: cooling usually matters less than host and thickness in straightforward cobalt blue
it matters more in floating-blue families where cobalt is only one part of the result
commonMistakes: raising cobalt when the host itself is dull
using oxide and carbonate as if they were equal-strength additions
ignoring how fast cobalt buries subtle breaking
stepSequence: test light, medium, and strong cobalt loads in the same host
compare one stable host and one reactive host
choose the weakest cobalt loading that still reads blue enough

families.name: Floating blue
families.goal: Get cream breaking, blue pools, and movement without losing all control.
bestHosts: reactive cone-6 hosts with moderate fluidity
natural-base floating-blue branches when a softer geological look is wanted
colourants: cobalt gives the blue body of the glaze
rutile and often tin help create the cream breaking and float effect
bodyEffects: lighter bodies give more obvious cream-and-blue separation
dark bodies can collapse the family into murky blue-brown
thicknessEffects: this family often needs more thickness than a stable liner to come alive
too much thickness turns the floating effect into pure run risk
coolingEffects: cooling can increase cream breaking and variegation
standard versus slow cool should be treated as part of the recipe
commonMistakes: testing only one coat count
making the host too stable and then expecting it to float
pushing cobalt when the real missing ingredient is movement
stepSequence: compare an engineered floating blue and a natural-base floating blue
run at least three thickness levels
document texture response on the same tile because break over relief is part of the look

families.name: Copper turquoise and green
families.goal: Keep oxidation copper bright instead of muddy, brown, or overly volatile.
bestHosts: clear glossy oxidation hosts
fluid gloss hosts only when dramatic pooling is part of the intent
colourants: copper usually wants a cleaner, more open host than iron does
higher copper is not the only route to stronger colour; host clarity matters more than many expect
bodyEffects: white bodies keep turquoise brighter
red or dark bodies push the colour toward green, brown, or a dirtier read
thicknessEffects: thicker copper often goes greener and darker
too much thickness can create crawling or a muddy sealed surface before the colour actually improves
coolingEffects: cooling usually changes depth more than hue in oxidation copper
very fluid hosts can show stronger pooling-based shifts
commonMistakes: adding more copper to a host that is already too opaque or too matte
treating food-surface questions as solved because the colour looks commercially familiar
using copper oxide at carbonate amounts
stepSequence: test a stable clear host and a fluid host side by side
use three copper levels rather than one heroic high load
reject the first version that begins to crawl or over-darken

families.name: Iron red and rust red
families.goal: Develop warm red iron surfaces without misreading brown as success.
bestHosts: reactive cone-6 hosts designed for iron red
high-fire iron gloss hosts when the target is darker and richer
colourants: iron red is a host-plus-schedule problem, not just a high-iron problem
the host must stay active enough to let iron develop as a surface event
bodyEffects: lighter bodies show the red shift more clearly
iron-rich bodies push the result darker and can hide whether the glaze is truly red
thicknessEffects: iron red usually needs enough thickness to develop but not enough to go muddy black-brown
thin application often reads as a disappointing amber or weak brown
coolingEffects: cooling is often decisive in whether the glaze reads red, rust, or just brown
document the schedule before changing the formula
commonMistakes: raising iron before controlling schedule and thickness
judging success from one tile only
using an overly stable host and expecting iron red to appear
stepSequence: use the reference iron-red host first
compare standard cool and slower cool on identical application thickness
only then test small iron adjustments

families.name: Chrome-tin pink and maroon
families.goal: Protect warm chrome-tin colour from the wrong host chemistry.
bestHosts: clear hosts chosen specifically for warm-colour development
clean white or porcelain bodies
colourants: chrome and tin can produce the famous pink route only in the right host window
the host must stay clean enough for the colour event to read
bodyEffects: white bodies matter more here than in darker iron families
warm or speckled bodies can make a good pink host look like a bad recipe
thicknessEffects: moderate, even coverage usually performs better than heavy pooling
too much opacity can choke the pink development
coolingEffects: cooling matters less than host choice and body colour in this family
schedule should still stay consistent while choosing the host
commonMistakes: testing the colour only in a general-purpose clear
letting zinc-bearing or overly modified hosts muddy the response
assuming more chrome or more tin solves a host problem
stepSequence: test the same pink addition in at least two clear hosts
use the lightest body available
lock the winning host before exploring shade changes

families.name: Oatmeal, cream, and soft white
families.goal: Build warm opaque or semi-opaque surfaces without drifting into dead beige.
bestHosts: silky or satin matte hosts
calcium or magnesia matte families with enough melt left in them
colourants: rutile, titanium, tin, and zircon all influence the warmth differently
a warm white often depends on host chemistry more than on one heavy opacifier
bodyEffects: warm bodies can make the result rich and earthy or simply dirty
white bodies show whether the recipe itself is elegant or only body-assisted
thicknessEffects: thin sections can keep some life while thick sections go oatmeal or stone
heavy opacity quickly kills depth if the host was already matte
coolingEffects: slow cooling often increases the soft buttery quality
fast cooling can keep the surface cleaner but less complex
commonMistakes: using zircon as a cheap tin replacement and expecting the same warmth
loading both rutile and opacity too heavily
choosing a base that is already too dry before adding cream-building ingredients
stepSequence: pick the host surface first: silky, satin, or matte
test one warmth source and one opacifier separately before combining them
promote the first version that keeps both softness and some depth

families.name: Black, smoke, and deep dark surfaces
families.goal: Separate transparent dark gloss, opaque black, and decorative smoke-black effects.
bestHosts: transparent iron hosts for temmoku-style depth
stain or opacity-assisted hosts for flatter, denser blacks
raku or smoke paths for decorative carbon-black results
colourants: iron, manganese, cobalt, chrome, stain, and carbon all create dark surfaces differently
a black glaze can be rich and transparent or dead and opaque depending on the path
bodyEffects: dark bodies make it harder to see whether the glaze itself has depth
light bodies reveal whether the black comes from the glaze or from the body underneath
thicknessEffects: more thickness often gives more black but less complexity
thin edges can reveal whether the glaze is actually a dark transparent family
coolingEffects: cooling matters most in iron-rich glossy darks
smoke-black decorative systems depend more on post-fire reduction than on kiln cooling
commonMistakes: calling every black result the same family
using opacity to hide a weak dark host instead of improving the base
treating decorative smoke black as a functional glaze path
stepSequence: decide whether the target is depth, opacity, or smoke effect
test a dark transparent, an opacified dark, and a decorative route separately
judge the edge transitions as carefully as the pooled black zones
